verses.text: After Hezekiah's faithful works, Sennacherib invades Judah, and the king wisely stops the water sources outside the city to deny the enemy. Faithfulness does not exempt us from trials. The godly meet danger with both prayer and prudent action, trusting God while doing their part.

After Hezekiah's faithful works, Sennacherib invades Judah, and the king wisely stops the water sources outside the city to deny the enemy. Faithfulness does not exempt us from trials. The godly meet danger with both prayer and prudent action, trusting God while doing their part.

Hezekiah repairs the wall, makes weapons and shields, sets captains over the people, and gathers them together. He prepares his city to stand. Faith is not idle; it strengthens the walls even while it leans wholly on the LORD.

Hezekiah repairs the wall, makes weapons and shields, sets captains over the people, and gathers them together. He prepares his city to stand. Faith is not idle; it strengthens the walls even while it leans wholly on the LORD.

He encourages them: 'be strong... there be more with us than with him: with him is an arm of flesh; but with us is the LORD our God to help us.' He weighs the unseen against the seen. The believer counts God on his side and finds the enemy's strength small.

He encourages them: 'be strong... there be more with us than with him: with him is an arm of flesh; but with us is the LORD our God to help us.' He weighs the unseen against the seen. The believer counts God on his side and finds the enemy's strength small.

Sennacherib's messengers taunt the city, asking on what they trust, and twisting Hezekiah's reforms as if removing the high places had offended God. The enemy mixes threat with deceit. Satan still slanders obedience itself, calling our faithfulness folly to shake our confidence.

The Assyrian boasts that no god of any nation could deliver its people from his hand, so why should the LORD? He lumps the living God with idols. The proud blasphemer cannot tell the difference between dead images and the God who made heaven and earth.

The Assyrian boasts that no god of any nation could deliver its people from his hand, so why should the LORD? He lumps the living God with idols. The proud blasphemer cannot tell the difference between dead images and the God who made heaven and earth.

The Assyrian boasts that no god of any nation could deliver its people from his hand, so why should the LORD? He lumps the living God with idols. The proud blasphemer cannot tell the difference between dead images and the God who made heaven and earth.

His servants speak and write yet more against the LORD God of Israel, railing as against the gods of the earth, the work of men's hands. Blasphemy reaches its height. To insult God to His face is to invite the very judgment that pride cannot foresee.

Hezekiah and Isaiah cry to heaven, and the LORD sends an angel who cuts off the Assyrian host; Sennacherib goes home ashamed and is slain in his own god's temple. Prayer routs the army no weapon could. The God who was mocked answers, and the boaster falls by the idol he trusted.

Hezekiah and Isaiah cry to heaven, and the LORD sends an angel who cuts off the Assyrian host; Sennacherib goes home ashamed and is slain in his own god's temple. Prayer routs the army no weapon could. The God who was mocked answers, and the boaster falls by the idol he trusted.

So the LORD saves Hezekiah and Jerusalem and guides them on every side, and many bring gifts, so the king is magnified among the nations. Deliverance brings honour. When God vindicates His people, the world that scorned them is made to see His hand.

So the LORD saves Hezekiah and Jerusalem and guides them on every side, and many bring gifts, so the king is magnified among the nations. Deliverance brings honour. When God vindicates His people, the world that scorned them is made to see His hand.

Hezekiah falls sick, prays, and is given a sign, but his heart is lifted up; wrath threatens, until he humbles himself and the judgment is delayed. Even a good man can swell with pride after mercy. Humbling restores favour; the contrite heart turns away the rod.

Hezekiah falls sick, prays, and is given a sign, but his heart is lifted up; wrath threatens, until he humbles himself and the judgment is delayed. Even a good man can swell with pride after mercy. Humbling restores favour; the contrite heart turns away the rod.

Hezekiah gains great riches and honour, builds storehouses, and brings the waters of Gihon down into the city; 'he prospered in all his works.' God blesses his diligence richly. Wisely managed, prosperity becomes a means of strength rather than a snare.

Hezekiah gains great riches and honour, builds storehouses, and brings the waters of Gihon down into the city; 'he prospered in all his works.' God blesses his diligence richly. Wisely managed, prosperity becomes a means of strength rather than a snare.

Hezekiah gains great riches and honour, builds storehouses, and brings the waters of Gihon down into the city; 'he prospered in all his works.' God blesses his diligence richly. Wisely managed, prosperity becomes a means of strength rather than a snare.

In the matter of the Babylonian envoys, God leaves Hezekiah to himself 'to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart.' Even the faithful are tested. God sometimes withdraws His felt help to show a man, and us, what is really within.

The rest of Hezekiah's acts and goodness are recorded; he dies and is honoured by all Judah at his death. A faithful reign closes in honour. The man who served God with all his heart is remembered and mourned by the people he led.
